Removal of AVB VLAN results in AVB going to non-operational. If MVRP is enabled globally, then AVB enable does not internally enable the static VLAN and waits for AVB VLAN to be created dynamically. If AVB is enabled first followed by MVRP, then the static VLAN is already created by AVB enable. In this scenario, AVB continues to operate with the static VLAN. input_text: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14, what does the guide say about Open Shortest Path First version 2 (OSPFv2)?
target_value: Open Shortest Path First version 2 (OSPFv2): Open Shortest Path First version 2 (OSPFv2) is a routing protocol described in RFC2328. It is a Link State- based IGP (Interior Gateway Protocol) routing protocol applied to routers grouped into OSPF areas identified by the routing configuration on each routing switch. It is widely used in medium to large-sized enterprise networks.

input_text: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14, what does the guide say about Open Shortest Path First version 3 (OSPFv3)?
target_value: Open Shortest Path First version 3 (OSPFv3): OSPFv3 is the IPv6 implementation of Open Shortest Path First protocol (OSPFv2 is the IPv4 implementation of this protocol). OSPFv3 is a routing protocol which is described in RFC5340 entitled OSPF for IPv6. It is a link-state based IGP (Interior Gateway Protocol) routing protocol. It is widely used with medium to large-sized enterprise networks.

input_text: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14, what does the guide say about Border Gateway Protocol (BGP)?
target_value: Border Gateway Protocol (BGP): BGP v4 (RFC 4271) is an interdomain path-vector routing protocol that runs over TCP on port 179. It provides a reliable loop-free routing between different Autonomous Systems (ASs). BGP routers (called speakers) form peerings (also known as neighbor relationships) with other BGP peers. These peerings can either be internal (iBGP: within the same AS) or external, (eBGP: connecting two different ASs).

Multicast Source Discovery Protocol (MSDP): Multicast Source Discovery Protocol (MSDP) is a mechanism to connect multiple Protocol Independent Multicast sparse mode (PIM-SM) domains. MSDP allows multicast sources for a group to be known to all rendezvous points (RPs) in different domains. An RP runs MSDP over TCP to discover multicast sources in other domains. The main advantage of MSDP is that it reduces the complexity of interconnecting multiple PIM-SM domains by allowing PIM-SM domains to use an interdomain source tree (rather than a common shared tree). Not supported on the Aruba 6200 Switch Series. input_text: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.14, how do you configure Static routes?
target_value: Static routes: Static multicast routing is not supported on the 9300 switch series. Route summarization is not supported on the 8400 and 9300 switch series. Static multicast routes can be configured by specifying the incoming interface, source, group, and outgoing interfaces. Multicast static routes can be used to configure a static multicast flow and program it directly into the IP multicast forwarding tables instead of running dynamic multicast protocols. Unlike PIM, this feature does not depend on Unicast Routing Protocols to build the Multicast tree. Instead, it works as a standalone Multicast Route provisioning feature that can interoperate with the dynamic Multicast protocols like PIM-SM and L2 protocols like IGMP.

Selecting a row in the VLANs list, displays more information on the VLAN in the details pane. Details include: VLAN mode, Admin State, Link State, and the associated LAG name. Use the Show/Hide Column Filters button or Column Settings button to customize the table display.
